title: What to know about Paeonia × suffruticosa (tree peony) before using it medicinally
section_medicine_ailments: Paeonia × suffruticosa helps healing several common ailments, such as inflammatory conditions, digestive issues, and emotional imbalances. One of its most notable benefits is in alleviating symptoms of arthritis, particularly osteoarthritis and rheumatoid arthritis, by reducing inflammation and relieving joint pain and stiffness. Additionally, the herb has been traditionally used to soothe menstrual cramps, bloating, and other symptoms associated with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) due to its ability to relax uterine muscles and regulate hormonal imbalances. Furthermore, Paeonia × suffruticosa has been shown to possess antispasmodic properties, making it effective in calming digestive issues such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS), bloating, and cramps. Its tranquilizing effects also make it a popular natural remedy for anxiety, insomnia, and stress-related disorders, promoting relaxation and reducing feelings of worry and unease.
section_medicine_properties: Paeonia × suffruticosa has several medicinal properties, such as anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and antispasmodic effects. The plant's root is particularly rich in paeoniflorin, a compound that has been shown to possess potent antioxidant activities, which can help to protect against oxidative stress and inflammation. Studies have demonstrated that extracts of Paeonia × suffruticosa can effectively reduce pain and inflammation in various animal models, including those with arthritis and muscle spasms. Additionally, the plant's ability to relax smooth muscles and reduce spasticity makes it a potential treatment for conditions such as irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) and urinary tract spasms. Furthermore, Paeonia × suffruticosa has also been shown to exhibit antimicrobial properties, which can help to prevent infections and promote wound healing. Overall, the medicinal properties of Paeonia × suffruticosa make it a valuable natural remedy for a range of health conditions, from pain management to infection prevention.
section_medicine_parts: The most commonly used parts of Paeonia × suffruticosa for medicinal purposes are its roots, rhizomes, and stems. These plant parts have been traditionally used in Chinese medicine to treat a range of health conditions, including rheumatism, arthritis, and gout, due to their rich content of bioactive compounds such as alkaloids, phenolic acids, and flavonoids. The roots are particularly prized for their ability to reduce inflammation and pain, while the rhizomes are used to treat digestive disorders and respiratory issues. The stems, meanwhile, are valued for their antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which have been shown to protect against cardiovascular disease and cancer. When processed into various preparations such as decoctions, tinctures, and powders, these plant parts can be used to alleviate symptoms of menopausal hot flashes, menstrual cramps, and skin conditions like eczema and acne, making Paeonia × suffruticosa a highly regarded herb in traditional Chinese medicine.
section_medicine_side_effects: When used improperly, Paeonia × suffruticosa increases the chances of experiencing side effects, such as allergic reactions, digestive issues, and interactions with other medications. One common issue is anaphylaxis, a severe and life-threatening allergic reaction that can occur when the herb is not processed or prepared correctly, leading to respiratory distress, rapid heartbeat, and even collapse. In addition, Paeonia × suffruticosa may cause gastrointestinal upset, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea, particularly if it is ingested in large quantities or without proper preparation. Furthermore, the herb has been known to interact with other medications, such as blood thinners, diabetes medications, and sedatives, which can lead to adverse reactions like increased bleeding risk, hypoglycemia, and excessive drowsiness. Moreover, Paeonia × suffruticosa may also cause sedation, dizziness, and confusion, especially when combined with other herbs or substances that have similar effects, potentially impairing cognitive function and coordination. As such, it is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using Paeonia × suffruticosa for medicinal purposes and to ensure proper preparation, processing, and dosage guidelines are followed to minimize the risk of adverse side effects.
section_medicine_precautions: The precautions to take before using Paeonia × suffruticosa medicinally are multifaceted and crucial for ensuring a safe and effective therapeutic outcome. Firstly, patients with known allergies or hypersensitivity to peonies should exercise extreme caution when consuming this herb, as anaphylactic reactions have been reported in rare cases. Additionally, pregnant women should avoid using Paeonia × suffruticosa altogether due to its potential to induce uterine contractions and stimulate menstruation, which may lead to miscarriage or premature labor. Furthermore, individuals with bleeding disorders, such as hemophilia, should consult their healthcare provider before taking this herb, as it may increase the risk of spontaneous bleeding due to its blood thinning properties. Moreover, patients receiving anticoagulant therapy should be closely monitored while taking Paeonia × suffruticosa, as its interactions with warfarin and other coumarin-based medications can lead to adverse effects such as bruising, hemorrhage, or increased risk of stroke. Finally, it is essential for individuals using Paeonia × suffruticosa medicinally to start with a low dose and gradually increase as needed, under the guidance of a qualified healthcare practitioner, to minimize the risk of adverse reactions and optimize therapeutic benefits.
section_horticulture: Paeonia × suffruticosa, commonly known as tree peony or Chinese tree peony, is a deciduous shrub that thrives in specific horticultural conditions. This perennial plant requires full sun to partial shade, with at least 6 hours of direct sunlight per day. It prefers well-draining soil that is rich in organic matter and has a slightly acidic pH, ranging from 6.0 to 7.0. The soil should be fertile but not waterlogged, as this can lead to root rot. Tree peonies are sensitive to extreme temperatures and moisture fluctuations, so they do best in areas with mild winters and cool summers. They require moderate watering, especially during the first growing season after planting, and benefit from regular mulching around their base to retain moisture and suppress weeds. Additionally, tree peonies need support as they grow, so providing a sturdy stake or trellis can help keep them upright and promote healthy growth. By meeting these horticultural conditions, gardeners can coax Paeonia × suffruticosa into producing stunning flowers that rival those of its cultivated cousins.
section_horticulture_growth: Paeonia × suffruticosa, a cross between Paeonia lactiflora and Paeonia officinalis, is a hybrid peony that thrives in well-draining soil with a slightly acidic to neutral pH (5.5-7.0). It requires full sun to partial shade, with at least six hours of direct sunlight per day for optimal flowering. The plant prefers rich, fertile soil with good organic matter content, and should be planted at a depth two to three times the size of its root ball. Adequate water supply is crucial, but Paeonia × suffruticosa is susceptible to rot if the soil is too wet or soggy. Mulching around the base of the plant helps retain moisture, suppress weeds, and regulate soil temperature. Regular fertilization with a balanced fertilizer in early spring and again after flowering can promote healthy growth and blooming. Deadheading spent flowers can encourage repeat blooms, while dividing every three to five years can help maintain the plant's vigor and prevent overcrowding. With proper care, Paeonia × suffruticosa can grow up to 3-4 feet tall and wide, making it a stunning addition to any garden or landscape.
section_horticulture_planting: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as Chinese peonies, require specific planting tips to thrive. When planting, choose a location that receives full sun to partial shade and has well-draining soil rich in organic matter. The roots of Paeonia × suffruticosa are sensitive to wet feet, so avoid planting in areas with standing water or where water tends to collect. Dig a hole twice as wide and just as deep as the root ball of the plant, and gently remove it from its container. Place the peony at the same depth it was previously growing, making sure the "eyes" (the small buds on the crown) are facing upwards. Fill the hole with soil, tamping it down gently to avoid compacting the roots. Water thoroughly after planting and mulch around the base of the plant to retain moisture and suppress weeds. It's also important to note that Paeonia × suffruticosa plants can take a few years to establish themselves before blooming, so be patient and enjoy the foliage and structural interest they provide in the meantime.
section_horticulture_caring: Paeonia × suffruticosa, a beautiful and delicate perennial herbaceous plant, requires special care to thrive. To promote healthy growth and blooming, it's essential to provide Paeonia × suffruticosa with well-draining soil that is rich in organic matter and has a slightly acidic pH. The plant prefers partial shade to full sun, but direct sunlight can scorch the leaves, so it's best to filter the sun or provide afternoon shade. Watering should be moderate, as excessive moisture can lead to root rot. Fertilization is crucial, using a balanced fertilizer during the growing season and avoiding over-fertilization. Pruning is also important, removing dead stems in the fall and cutting back foliage after blooming to encourage new growth. Finally, protect Paeonia × suffruticosa from extreme temperatures, pests, and diseases by providing a layer of mulch around the base of the plant and monitoring for any signs of distress. By following these caring tips, you can enjoy the stunning flowers and attractive foliage of this lovely plant.
section_horticulture_harvesting: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as Chinese peony, requires careful harvesting to ensure that its delicate and beautiful flowers remain intact. The best time to harvest Paeonia × suffruticosa is in late spring or early summer when the buds have fully opened and the petals are still slightly tender. To avoid damaging the stems, cut them at an angle using sharp scissors or pruning shears, leaving a small portion of stem attached to the plant. It's essential to handle the flowers gently, as they are prone to bruising and tearing. Avoid washing the cut stems immediately after harvesting, as this can cause further damage and reduce their vase life. Instead, place them in a bucket of warm water with a floral preservative added, and then trim the stems underwater before arranging. With proper care, Paeonia × suffruticosa blooms can last up to 7-10 days in a vase.
section_horticulture_pests_diseases: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as Chinese peony, is a beautiful perennial plant that is susceptible to several pests and diseases. One of the most common pests affecting this species is the aphid, which can cause curled or distorted leaves and stunted growth. The caterpillar of the peony moth is another significant pest that feeds on the leaves and flowers, causing damage and reducing yields. Fungal diseases such as botrytis and fusarium wilt can also infect Paeonia × suffruticosa, causing symptoms like leaf spots, blight, and root rot. Additionally, powdery mildew is a common problem that can cover the leaves with a white, powdery coating, reducing photosynthesis and plant vigor. Root-knot nematodes are another soil-borne pest that can cause galls to form on the roots, disrupting water and nutrient uptake. If left unchecked, these pests and diseases can significantly impact the health and productivity of Paeonia × suffruticosa, making it essential for gardeners and farmers to implement integrated pest management strategies to control infestations and infections.
section_botany: Paeonia × suffruticosa is a perennial flowering plant that belongs to the family Paeoniaceae. The botanical characteristics of this species are quite distinct, with its shrub-like habit and dense foliage. The stems of Paeonia × suffruticosa are woody at the base, becoming herbaceous towards the top, growing up to 2-3 feet tall. The leaves are large, lobed or deeply toothed, and typically range in color from dark green to blue-green. Each leaf can grow up to 12 inches long and 6 inches wide, providing a lush, full appearance. The plant produces showy flowers that bloom in the late spring to early summer, featuring delicate, petal-like sepals and numerous pistils. The flowers are typically pink or white in color, with some varieties exhibiting subtle purple or reddish undertones. Overall, Paeonia × suffruticosa boasts an impressive display of botanical characteristics, making it a popular choice among gardeners and plant enthusiasts alike.
section_botany_taxonomy: Paeonia × suffruticosa belongs to the kingdom Plantae, class Magnoliopsida, order Fabales, family Paeoniaceae, and is classified as a hybrid species within the genus Paeonia. It is a cross between Paeonia lactiflora and Paeonia officinalis, two Asian species of peony. This classification reflects its botanical relationship to other flowering plants and its distinct characteristics that set it apart from other plant species.
section_botany_variants: Paeonia × suffruticosa has variants, such as 'Bath's Pink', 'Coral Charm', 'Festiva Maxima', 'Garden Pearl', and 'Red Charm', which are popular cultivars of the hybrid peony. These variants offer a range of flower colors, including pink, coral, white, red, and bi-colored, and differ in their foliage and plant growth habits. Some variants, such as 'Festiva Maxima', have large, double flowers with intricate petals, while others, like 'Garden Pearl', produce smaller, semi-double blooms. These variations allow gardeners to choose the perfect peony for their landscape or bouquet needs.
section_botany_distribution: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as rock peony, is a hybrid species of peony that originated in China and is now widely cultivated for its ornamental value. Its geographic distribution includes eastern Asia, primarily China, Japan, and Korea, where it grows in woodland edges, rocky outcrops, and mountainous regions at elevations up to 2,000 meters. It has also been introduced to Europe and North America, where it is often grown as an ornamental plant in gardens and parks. Despite its limited natural range, Paeonia × suffruticosa has become a popular garden subject due to its striking flowers and attractive foliage.
section_botany_life_cycle: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as Chinese peony or tree peony, is a perennial plant that completes its life cycle over several years. The process begins in early spring when new growth emerges from the crown of the plant. As the weather warms up, the stems elongate and produce large showy flowers with intricate petals. After flowering, the plant enters a period of vegetative growth, producing leaves that provide food for the roots to store energy for next year's bloom cycle. In late summer or early fall, the foliage dies back, and the plant goes dormant until the following spring. Over time, Paeonia × suffruticosa can grow into a mature, woody shrub-like plant that requires minimal maintenance and can thrive for decades with proper care.
section_history: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as tree peony, has a rich history dating back over 2,000 years. In ancient China, its showy flowers were highly prized for their beauty and fragrance, used in traditional medicine to treat ailments such as fever and rheumatism. The plant's roots and leaves were also used as a dye, giving fabrics a vibrant yellow color. In Chinese culture, tree peonies are considered a symbol of good fortune and prosperity, making them a popular ornamental choice for gardens and temples.
section_history_mythology: Paeonia × suffruticosa, a hybrid peony species, is often referred to as "Feng Dong" in Chinese culture. According to mythological references, the plant's beauty and fragrance are said to have been favored by the goddess of mercy, Guanyin. In ancient Greek mythology, the peony was associated with Paeon, the god of medicine, and was believed to possess healing properties. These mythological associations have contributed to the plant's enduring symbolism in various cultures, representing good fortune, prosperity, and a long, healthy life.
section_history_simbology: Paeonia × suffruticosa, also known as tree peony, has been imbued with rich symbolic meanings across cultures. In Chinese culture, it represents good fortune, prosperity, and a long, happy life. Its large, showy flowers are said to bring joy and happiness to those who behold them. In some Eastern European traditions, the tree peony is associated with modesty, shame, and bashfulness due to its shy blooming habit.
section_history_literature: Paeonia × suffruticosa, a hybrid of Paeonia lactiflora and Paeonia officinalis, has been cultivated for centuries in East Asia. Historical texts such as "Ben Cao Gang Mu" by Li Shizhen (1578) and "Pen-ts'ao Ching" by Shen Nong (2800 BCE) mention its medicinal properties, used to treat a range of ailments including rheumatism and snake bites. These ancient texts also describe the plant's ornamental value, with illustrations depicting its showy flowers and lush foliage.
section_history_artifacts: Paeonia × suffruticosa, a hybrid peony species, has a rich history dating back to ancient China. Historical artifacts related to this plant include ancient Chinese paintings and scrolls depicting its beautiful flowers, as well as ceramic vases and figurines featuring the peony as a symbol of good fortune and prosperity. These artifacts showcase the significance of Paeonia × suffruticosa in Chinese culture and art for centuries.
intro_desc: Paeonia × suffruticosa, commonly known as tree peony, is a herbaceous perennial with a multitude of health-promoting properties, including anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects, which have been shown to improve symptoms of arthritis and reduce oxidative stress. In terms of horticultural aspects, Paeonia × suffruticosa requires a well-drained soil and full sun to partial shade, making it a versatile and adaptable addition to gardens and landscapes. Botanically, this herb belongs to the Paeoniaceae family and is known for its large, showy flowers and robust foliage. Historically, Paeonia × suffruticosa has been used in traditional Chinese medicine for centuries, and is still highly prized for its medicinal and ornamental value.
medicine_desc: Paeonia × suffruticosa helps with relieving pain and inflammation due to its analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties. It is used to treat arthritis, gout, and menstrual cramps. Its extracts are also used to lower blood pressure and cholesterol levels.

The active constituents of Paeonia × suffruticosa include paeonol, paeonol glycosides, and benzaldehyde, which are responsible for its medicinal properties. These compounds have been shown to exhibit anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and cardiovascular protective effects.

The most commonly used parts of the Paeonia × suffruticosa plant for medicinal purposes are the roots and flowers. The roots are rich in paeonol and are used to treat fever, rheumatism, and inflammation. The flowers are used to treat menstrual disorders, such as dysmenorrhea and amenorrhea.

Improper use of Paeonia × suffruticosa can cause side effects such as gastrointestinal upset, allergic reactions, and bleeding disorders. Prolonged use of high doses can lead to kidney and liver damage.

Precautions when using Paeonia × suffruticosa medicinally include consulting a healthcare professional, especially for pregnant or breastfeeding women, children, and individuals with bleeding disorders or taking anticoagulant medications. Dosage and preparation should also be carefully monitored to avoid adverse effects.
horticulture_desc: Paeonia × suffruticosa grow best in full sun to partial shade and well-drained soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0. They require a cool winter period to induce flowering and can tolerate temperatures as low as -20°C. Soil moisture should be consistent, but not waterlogged.

Planting should be done in fall or early spring, when the plant is dormant. Dig a hole twice as wide and as deep as the root system. Mix in compost or fertilizer to improve soil fertility. Plant the tree peony at the same depth as it was in the pot, and water well.

Harvesting tree peony flowers typically occurs in late spring, when the buds have opened fully. Cut off the stems at an angle, just above a leaf node, to encourage new growth. Deadhead spent flowers to promote further blooming. Cut flowers can be arranged in bouquets or used in floral arrangements.

Tree peony is susceptible to pests such as Japanese beetle, aphids, and caterpillars. Diseases that commonly affect tree peony include powdery mildew, botrytis, and root rot. Regular monitoring and treatment with insecticides or fungicides can help control these issues.
